title: Touch Actions & Gestures
overview: Simulate mobile gestures using Appium's W3C Actions API (Appium 2) and legacy TouchAction (Appium 1). Gestures include tap, longPress, swipe, scroll, pinch-to-zoom.
whyItMatters: Mobile apps rely heavily on touch interactions. Without gestures, you cannot test core functionalities like pull-to-refresh, swiping carousels, or drag-and-drop.
explanation: In Appium 1, `TouchAction` was used, but it's deprecated. Appium 2 embraces the W3C Actions API (`PointerInput` and `Sequence`) for building complex touch gestures. You define pointer events (pointerDown, pointerMove, pointerUp) and combine them into a sequence to perform on the driver. Alternatively, Appium provides mobile-specific execute scripts (e.g., `mobile: swipeGestures`) via plugins to simplify common gestures.
keyPoints: W3C Actions API uses `PointerInput` for simulating fingers.
A swipe is pointer down -> pointer move -> pointer up.
`mobile: swipe` and `mobile: scroll` are convenient alternatives.
realWorldExample: Testing a dating app requires a 'swipe right' gesture. You create a W3C sequence that moves the pointer from the center of the screen to the right edge over 500 milliseconds.
commonMistakes: Using the deprecated `TouchAction` class in new Appium 2 frameworks.
Hardcoding screen coordinates instead of calculating them dynamically based on screen size.
bestPractices: Calculate swipe coordinates dynamically using `driver.manage().window().getSize()` to support different screen resolutions.
Use mobile-specific extensions (`driver.executeScript("mobile: swipe", ImmutableMap.of(...))`) when available for simpler code.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you perform a swipe in Appium 2?
interviewQuestions.a: Using the W3C Actions API with `PointerInput` and `Sequence`, simulating a pointerDown, pointerMove with a duration, and pointerUp. Or using the `mobile: swipe` execute script.

revisionSummary: Mobile gestures (swipe, zoom) are performed using the W3C Actions API in Appium 2. Calculate coordinates dynamically to ensure cross-device compatibility, and avoid deprecated TouchAction classes.
